main_summary.summary: Robert L. Carter, born 1917 in Caryville, Florida, recounts his journey from Newark and East Orange childhood through Lincoln University (where he debated and agitated for black faculty), Howard University School of Law (entered 1937 on scholarship under dean William H. Hastie), and Columbia, where his Rosenwald Fellowship thesis under Noel Dowling addressed First Amendment strike rights. Carter describes attending Marian Anderson's 1939 Lincoln Memorial concert after being refused at Constitution Hall by the DAR, his Army Air Corps service (which radicalized him against segregation through encounters with a Lieutenant Colonel and the officers' club), and his recruitment by Hastie to Thurgood Marshall's NAACP staff in 1944. He argued his first Supreme Court case in McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents, worked alongside Marshall, William T. Coleman Jr., Spottswood Robinson, and Constance Baker Motley, and recruited Kenneth and Mamie Clark — after Otto Klineberg's referral — whose doll test grounded the Clarendon County (Briggs) case and the Brown v. Board victory. Carter became NAACP General Counsel in 1955, defended the organization in NAACP v. Alabama and NAACP v. Button against southern attempts to outlaw it, attacked northern school segregation through Ocean Hill-Brownsville and Intermediate School 201, and resigned in 1968 over the firing of Lewis M. Steel. He later became a federal judge.
key_themes: social science evidence in constitutional litigation
Howard Law School and Charles Hamilton Houston's program
NAACP defense against southern outlawing campaigns
main_summary.historical_significance: Carter is the primary surviving architect of the NAACP Legal Defense Fund litigation strategy that produced Brown v. Board of Education. His testimony documents the intellectual lineage from Charles Hamilton Houston's separate-but-equal exhaustion strategy through the McLaurin and Sweatt precedents to the per-se-unconstitutional argument that prevailed in Brown, and his account of recruiting Kenneth and Mamie Clark provides first-person attribution of the doll-test evidence that grounded Chief Justice Warren's unanimous opinion. The interview is also a primary source on the NAACP's defense of its own organizational existence after Brown (NAACP v. Alabama, NAACP v. Button), Carter's pivot to northern de facto segregation cases including Ocean Hill-Brownsville and Intermediate School 201, his 1968 resignation over the firing of attorney Lewis M. Steel, and the internal post-Marshall power struggle with Jack Greenberg at the LDF.

chapters.title: Born in Caryville, Newark Migration
chapters.summary: Carter recounts his 1917 birth in Caryville, Florida and his family's participation in the Great Migration to Newark, where his father had been sent north to work in a factory. His father died when Carter was one year old. He describes growing up in poverty with his mother taking in washing for white families until his older sister married a Linden, New Jersey bar owner when Carter was twelve, which moved the family out of the Newark slums.

chapters.title: Schools and Race in Newark and East Orange
chapters.summary: Carter describes how he gained awareness of race through a sixth-grade incident where a teacher told a white girl who had befriended a black classmate to 'stay with your own kind.' He contrasts Newark schools, which let racial conflict play out, with East Orange schools, where teachers actively enforced separation. He recounts integrating the school swimming pool at Barringer High School by entering it alone after a state Supreme Court ruling, leading administrators to close the pool entirely rather than open it.

chapters.title: Lincoln University and Debate
chapters.summary: Enrolling at Lincoln University at sixteen, Carter describes the all-male, predominantly black school as isolated and a great time in his life. He participated in the debating team against Haverford and other small colleges, which taught him he could compete with white students. He and his generation agitated for more black faculty, since only one black English professor was on staff at the time.

chapters.title: Howard Law School Under Houston
chapters.summary: Carter entered Howard University School of Law on scholarship in 1937. He took William H. Hastie as his model — a soft-spoken but brilliant dean — and was drawn into Charles Hamilton Houston's program to attack Plessy v. Ferguson by forcing states to either build separate facilities or accept black students. He witnessed Houston argue Missouri ex rel. Gaines before the Supreme Court and watched Justice James Clark McReynolds turn his back throughout the argument, the case that established the moot-court-night-before tradition.

chapters.title: Marian Anderson at the Lincoln Memorial
chapters.summary: Carter recounts Easter Sunday 1939, when he was working as a waiter to supplement his scholarship. After the DAR refused Marian Anderson permission to sing at Constitution Hall and Eleanor Roosevelt arranged for her to perform at the Lincoln Memorial instead, Carter walked off the job to attend. He describes the large integrated crowd in tears and was fired upon his return, but subsequently received a full scholarship plus a library work-study position — making the firing a net gain.

chapters.title: Columbia and the Rosenwald Fellowship
chapters.summary: After Howard, Carter went to Columbia on a Rosenwald Fellowship to work toward a doctorate on First Amendment strike rights. He chose Noel Dowling as his advisor — a Columbia constitutional law specialist who taught Carter to argue narrow technical distinctions even while believing Supreme Court rulings actually tracked political change. Carter learned the technique of giving the court a narrow path to rule for his side without making sweeping declarations.

chapters.title: Army Service and the Officers' Club
chapters.summary: Drafted in 1941, Carter served in the Army Air Corps and was sent to Augusta, GA to clear brush. After becoming a personal legal advisor to his ignorant staff sergeant, he attended Officer Candidate School and became a Second Lieutenant. He recounts confronting a Lieutenant Colonel who called him a slur over a clothes hanger, the officers' club incident where a black bartender feared to serve him until a white officer put the drink on his bill, and the racism that made a militant of him.

chapters.title: Joining the NAACP Under Marshall
chapters.summary: Hastie recruited Carter to Thurgood Marshall's NAACP staff after his 1944 discharge. Initially given a small office while the rest of the staff worked in a big room, Carter was assigned a brief on the separate-but-equal doctrine during Marshall's long lunch. Marshall accepted Carter's brief unchanged and sent it out, beginning Carter's role as the behind-the-scenes brief writer. Carter describes the discomfort of writing for years without recognition before insisting on credit.

chapters.title: Traveling the South and McLaurin
chapters.summary: Carter describes traveling the South for NAACP cases when there were no hotel facilities for black lawyers, sometimes staying with black families. He recounts a Mississippi teacher-salary case where Constance Baker Motley — the first black woman lawyer the local community had seen — co-argued, and the courtroom audience reenacted the questioning in barbershops and beauty parlors. His first Supreme Court argument was McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents, where the segregation consisted of George W. McLaurin being required to sit at a separate table and seat despite same faculty and books. Carter argued segregation itself denied equal opportunity.

chapters.title: Direct Attack on Plessy and the Clarks
chapters.summary: In June 1950 the NAACP lawyers shifted strategy to attack Plessy v. Ferguson directly while retaining unequal-facilities as a backup. Preparing the Clarendon County, South Carolina case (Briggs v. Elliott), Carter sought out Otto Klineberg at Columbia — chief social scientist there — who declined to testify but referred Carter to Kenneth and Mamie (Phipps) Clark. The Clarks' doll test, showing black children identified the white doll as 'good' and the black doll as 'bad,' became the only case in which the test was used in direct presentation. Spottswood Robinson was initially skeptical when he saw Carter carrying dolls through the Charleston train station.
related_events: Briggs v. Elliott Clarendon County case

chapters.title: Brown v. Board and All Deliberate Speed
chapters.summary: Carter describes social science as the key to Brown — initially resisted by William T. Coleman Jr. and others as not hard science, but later agreed to be excellent. He explains that the Topeka, Kansas case was the lead case argued on segregation per se. Carter warned that 'all deliberate speed' would allow decades of delay, which proved correct as it took ten to twenty years before Topeka schools actually desegregated. The significance of Brown to him was the constitutional holding that enforced segregation by law was no longer viable, even if voluntary segregation persisted.

chapters.title: Defending the NAACP from Southern Attack
chapters.summary: After Brown, southern states attempted to outlaw the NAACP through membership-disclosure laws. Carter spent years in a 'grueling spree' defending the organization in NAACP v. Alabama (protecting membership lists) and NAACP v. Button (protecting the right of civil rights organizations to seek educational opportunity through litigation). The First Amendment associational-rights work from his Columbia thesis became directly load-bearing.

chapters.title: Greenberg, the LDF Split, and Resignation
chapters.summary: Carter recounts becoming NAACP General Counsel in 1955 and his conflict with Jack Greenberg, who, after Thurgood Marshall's appointment to the Second Circuit Court of Appeals, sought to have Roy Wilkins bar Carter from bringing segregation cases — directing them all to the now-separate Legal Defense Fund. Carter held onto cases already in hand. In 1968, after the board fired NAACP staff attorney Lewis M. Steel without notice over a published article, Carter and his entire staff resigned in protest. He briefly recounts being approached by Adam Clayton Powell Jr. for money in exchange for a judgeship — which Carter refused — and credits the resulting fight for Marshall's later Second Circuit appointment.

chapters.title: Northern Segregation and Obama Reflection
chapters.summary: Carter argues segregation was a national phenomenon, not a southern problem. He took on Ocean Hill-Brownsville and Intermediate School 201 cases to attack northern de facto school segregation. He observes voluntary segregation persists with no public push to end it, hoping a movement will eventually arise. Asked about Obama's 2008 election, Carter says his focus was always equal educational opportunity rather than a black presidency, and describes himself as too old and mentally fatigued to do further substantive work, content to leave the next push to younger people.
